prompt.system:
You are a legal research assistant specialising in formal methods
applied to law. Your answers must be structured to support formalisation in Lean 4
dependent type theory. You are helping build a library called LegalLean that encodes
legal reasoning with machine-checkable guarantees.

CRITICAL: Structure your response as JSON with the following schema. Do NOT return
prose — return structured data that maps to type definitions.

Be exhaustive about conditions, exceptions, and vagueness. Legal formalisation
requires knowing EVERY rule, exception, and point of interpretive discretion.
prompt.user:
Compare how the following legal formalisation systems handle
dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants). Return a JSON object:

{
  "dimension": "dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants)",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "string — system name",
      "approach": "string — how it handles this dimension",
      "strengths": ["string"],
      "limitations": ["string"],
      "key_paper": "string — citation",
      "expressiveness_score": "1-5 (1=absent, 5=fully native)"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "string — where dependent types in Lean 4 add value",
  "legallean_limitation": "string — honest assessment of gaps"
}

Systems to compare: DDL (Governatori), Catala (Merigoux), L4L (Hou et al.),
LegalRuleML (Palmirani), LegalLean (our system — Lean 4 dependent types with
FormalisationBoundary for open texture).

Dimension: dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants)
Context: Whether the system can express that evidence INHABITS a type (propositions-as-types / Curry-Howard). Include: proof witnesses.

responses.answer_raw:
```json
{
  "dimension": "dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants)",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "DDL (Governatori)",
      "approach": "Defeasible logic with no dependent types or Curry-Howard correspondence; rules are flat horn clauses with priorities for exceptions, evidence handled as rule application traces rather than type inhabitants.",
      "strengths": ["Efficient operational semantics for defeasible reasoning", "Handles legal exceptions via superiority relations"],
      "limitations": ["No propositions-as-types", "Evidence not encoded as constructive proofs", "Cannot express dependent propositions where evidence refines types"],
      "key_paper": "Governatori, G. (2005). Representing business contracts in RuleML. International Symposium on RuleML.",
      "expressiveness_score": "1"
    },
    {
      "name": "Catala (Merigoux)",
      "approach": "Rule-based DSL compiled to OCaml with scope/attribute hierarchies for tax law; no dependent types, evidence via computation traces and tests, not type-level proofs.",
      "strengths": ["Precise replication of statutory ambiguity", "Compiles to executable code", "Strong testing framework"],
      "limitations": ["Static types are simple ML types", "No Curry-Howard", "Legal discretion not type-refined", "Evidence external to types"],
      "key_paper": "Merigoux, D., et al. (2022). Catala: a domain-specific language for tax law. Proceedings of the ACM on Programming Languages.",
      "expressiveness_score": "1"
    },
    {
      "name": "L4L (Hou et al.)",
      "approach": "Lean 4 framework translating natural language to Lean theorems; uses Lean's full dependent types and Curry-Howard for legal proofs, with LLM-assisted formalisation.",
      "strengths": ["Full Lean 4 dependent types", "Native evidence-as-inhabitants", "Machine-checkable legal proofs"],
      "limitations": ["Relies on LLM accuracy for NL→Lean", "Scalability of full formalisation", "Limited to what Lean can express"],
      "key_paper": "Hou, K., et al. (2024). Large Language Models as Legal Proof Assistants. arXiv:2406.11585.",
      "expressiveness_score": "5"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalRuleML (Palmirani)",
      "approach": "XML-based rule markup with defeasibility and deontic operators; no type system or dependent types, evidence via rule firing traces in compliant engines.",
      "strengths": ["Standardized XML interchange", "Supports defeasibility", "Wide tool ecosystem"],
      "limitations": ["No type theory foundation", "Evidence not computationally constructive", "Semantic variation across engines", "No dependent propositions"],
      "key_paper": "Palmirani, M., et al. (2011). LegalRuleML: Design principles and foundations. International Conference on Electronic Government and the Information Systems Perspective.",
      "expressiveness_score": "1"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalLean (Lean 4)",
      "approach": "Native Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary; full Curry-Howard where legal evidence inhabits proposition types, open texture via boundary predicates between formal/interpretive layers.",
      "strengths": ["Machine-checkable legal proofs", "Dependent propositions encode case-specific facts", "FormalisationBoundary handles vagueness"],
      "limitations": ["Steep learning curve", "Partial formalisation requires boundary judgment", "Lean elaboration can be brittle"],
      "key_paper": "LegalLean: Dependent Types for Legal Reasoning (in development)",
      "expressiveness_score": "5"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "Lean 4's dependent types enable precise encoding of 'evidence inhabits legal propositions' via Curry-Howard, supporting case-specific refinements (e.g., {proof : is_contract_valid doc} → obligation_holds) and FormalisationBoundary theorems that compose machine-checked cores with interpretive discretion.",
  "legallean_limitation": "Full dependent type formalisation scales poorly to complete statutes; FormalisationBoundary decisions remain human judgments requiring legal expertise, creating trusted oracles between formal and interpretive layers."
}
```

systems.name: DDL (Governatori)
systems.approach: Defeasible logic with no dependent types or Curry-Howard correspondence; rules are flat horn clauses with priorities for exceptions, evidence handled as rule application traces rather than type inhabitants.
strengths: Efficient operational semantics for defeasible reasoning
Handles legal exceptions via superiority relations
limitations: Evidence not encoded as constructive proofs
Cannot express dependent propositions where evidence refines types
systems.key_paper: Governatori, G. (2005). Representing business contracts in RuleML. International Symposium on RuleML.

systems.name: Catala (Merigoux)
systems.approach: Rule-based DSL compiled to OCaml with scope/attribute hierarchies for tax law; no dependent types, evidence via computation traces and tests, not type-level proofs.
limitations: Static types are simple ML types
systems.key_paper: Merigoux, D., et al. (2022). Catala: a domain-specific language for tax law. Proceedings of the ACM on Programming Languages.

systems.name: L4L (Hou et al.)
systems.approach: Lean 4 framework translating natural language to Lean theorems; uses Lean's full dependent types and Curry-Howard for legal proofs, with LLM-assisted formalisation.
limitations: Relies on LLM accuracy for NL→Lean
Limited to what Lean can express
systems.key_paper: Hou, K., et al. (2024). Large Language Models as Legal Proof Assistants. arXiv:2406.11585.

systems.name: LegalRuleML (Palmirani)
systems.approach: XML-based rule markup with defeasibility and deontic operators; no type system or dependent types, evidence via rule firing traces in compliant engines.
systems.key_paper: Palmirani, M., et al. (2011). LegalRuleML: Design principles and foundations. International Conference on Electronic Government and the Information Systems Perspective.

systems.name: LegalLean (Lean 4)
systems.approach: Native Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary; full Curry-Howard where legal evidence inhabits proposition types, open texture via boundary predicates between formal/interpretive layers.
systems.key_paper: LegalLean: Dependent Types for Legal Reasoning (in development)

answer_parsed.legallean_advantage: Lean 4's dependent types enable precise encoding of 'evidence inhabits legal propositions' via Curry-Howard, supporting case-specific refinements (e.g., {proof : is_contract_valid doc} → obligation_holds) and FormalisationBoundary theorems that compose machine-checked cores with interpretive discretion.
answer_parsed.legallean_limitation: Full dependent type formalisation scales poorly to complete statutes; FormalisationBoundary decisions remain human judgments requiring legal expertise, creating trusted oracles between formal and interpretive layers.

consensus.note: Manual review needed to synthesise responses into final argument
